m_Item1.QuestionText:
Consider following singly linked-list:
 10->20->30->40
  If head is pointing to node 10, then how will you display 30 ?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Link List every node is holding address of next node so, head->next will give give you  address of node containg value 20 , head->next->next will give you address of node 30 so head->next->next->data will give you  value 30.

Options.OptionText: for relatively permanent collections of data
for the size of the structure and the data in the structure are constantly changing

m_Item1.Explanation: Linked list can grow & shrink(change the size) dynamically at run time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What kind of list is best to answer questions such as 'What is the item at position n?'

Options.OptionText: Doubly-linked or singly-linked lists are equally best

m_Item1.Explanation: In array we can directly access array element on any specific index.

m_Item1.QuestionText: The operation of processing each element in the list is known as
m_Item1.Explanation: Processing each element is nothing but visiting or traversing the Link List

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following operations is performed more efficiently by doubly linked list as compared to singly linked list ?

Options.OptionText: Deleting a node whose location in given
Searching in an unsorted list for a given item
Inverting a node after the node with given location
Traversing a list to process each node

m_Item1.Explanation: A Doubly Linked List (DLL) contains an extra pointer, typically called previous pointer, together with next pointer and data which are there in singly linked list.So When we  want to delete node from Doubly Linklist we need to manage two pointers. Where as in singly linklist only one pointer  we have to manage.

m_Item1.QuestionText: A linear collection of data elements where the linear node is given by means of pointer is called
m_Item1.Explanation: In Linked list each node has its own data and the address of next node. These nodes are linked by using pointers. Node list is an object that consists of a list of all nodes in a document with in a particular selected set of nodes.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Consider a linked list of n elements. What is the time taken to insert an element after element pointed by same pointer?
m_Item1.Explanation: In Linklist each and every element is dynamically allocated and every previous node is holding address of next node so time taken to insert node after any node pointed by some pointer need o(1) time.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In chaining, what data structure is appropriate?
m_Item1.Explanation: chaining is just like as sequence and addition and deletion becomes easy in singly linked list so signly linklist is appropriate data structure for chaining.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is not a type of Linked List ?
m_Item1.Explanation: There is nothing called as hybrid linked list.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Linked list is generally considered as an example of _________ type of memory allocation
m_Item1.Explanation: Linked lists are dynamic allocations , done on the heap section of memory.
